sentences.base_language: I need to go to the hospital.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜야 하다 / 〜아야 하다 (ya hada) = 'must / have to': 가야 해요 (must go), 먹어야 해요 (must eat). It's the standard way to express obligation in Korean. The negative 〜지 않아도 되다 means 'don't have to'.

**어디가 아파요?** = 'where does it hurt?' — the subject marker **가** follows 어디 (where) to mark the hurting location as subject.
**아프다** (apeuda) = 'to hurt / to be sick / to be in pain' — a key medical adjective. Body part + 이/가 + 아파요 is the standard pain pattern: 머리가 아파요 (my head hurts), 배가 아파요 (my stomach hurts).
Doctors in Korea often ask 어디가 아프세요? using the honorific -세요 to show respect to the patient.

sentences.base_language: My throat hurts and I have a fever.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜고 (go) connects two clauses as a list or sequence: 아프고 (hurts and...), 피곤하고 (tired and...). It's neutral — it doesn't imply cause or contrast, just addition. Body part + 이/가 + 아프다 is the core pain expression pattern.

sentences.base_language: Since when have you been sick?
sentences.grammarNote:
**언제부터** (eonjebuteo) = 'from when / since when' — 언제 (when) + 부터 (from/since).
This is a common medical question: 언제부터 아팠어요? = 'since when have you been sick?'
**부터** (buteo) marks a starting point in time: 어제부터 (since yesterday), 아침부터 (since this morning).
The past tense **아팠어요** (aposseoyo) comes from 아프다 → 아파 (irregular 으 drop) → 아팠어요.

부터 (buteo) = 'from / since' (a starting point in time): 어제부터 (since yesterday), 아침부터 (since morning). Its counterpart 까지 (kkaji) = 'until / up to': 내일까지 (until tomorrow). Together: 월요일부터 금요일까지 (Monday through Friday).

〜아/어 주세요 (a/eo juseyo) = 'please do [something] for me'. It softens a request by framing the action as a favor: 벌려 주세요 (please open [it]), 가르쳐 주세요 (please teach me). This is the standard polite request form in Korean.

sentences.base_language: It's a cold. I'll give you medicine.
sentences.grammarNote: 드리다 (deurida) is the humble form of 주다 (to give) — used when the speaker gives something to someone of higher status: 드릴게요 (I will give [to you/them]). This shows respect to the patient as the recipient.

sentences.base_language: Pick up the medicine at the pharmacy.
sentences.grammarNote:
**받다** (batda) = 'to receive / to pick up' — here used for collecting a prescription: 약을 받다 (receive/pick up medicine).
**약국** (yakguk) = pharmacy — 약 (medicine) + 국 (局, office/bureau). In Korea, pharmacies are separate from clinics — you take your prescription there after seeing a doctor.
**에서** marks the place of action: 약국에서 (at the pharmacy).
**-으세요** is the honorific imperative: 받으세요 = 'please receive / please pick up' — politely instructing the patient.
